metadata.title: A View From Somewhere: Human-Centric Face Representations
organizations: Sony AI, Tokyo; University of Tokyo, Tokyo; Sony AI, New York

sections.title: Motivation
content: Dataset Bias: Dataset bias can result in models that are discriminatory or depend on spurious correlations. For instance, a face image restoration model may "hallucinate" features correlated with oversampled subgroups, resulting in the erasure of minority groups.
Prior to learning, human-centric image dataset users shouldevaluate the diversity of the data.

figures.description: Upsampling via latent space exploration of StyleGAN2 - Example 1 showing downsampling and upsampling artifacts
Upsampling via latent space exploration of StyleGAN2 - Example 2 showing demographic feature shifts

sections.title: Parity and Diversity
content: The canonical approach is to categorize people using demographic labels. However, evaluating diversity by examining counts across subgroupsfails to reflect the continuous nature of human phenotypic diversity.

figures.description: Visualization of subgroup representation versus actual visual diversity within a demographic subgroup

sections.title: Unknown Label Distributions
content: Most human-centric image datasets are web-scraped, lacking ground-truth information about the image subjects. Observational labels risk not only encoding stereotypes, but reifying and propagating them beyond their "cultural context".

figures.description: Diagram showing an unlabeled image being processed by an annotator to produce a structured JSON of perceived attributes

sections.title: Contributions
content: Motivated by issues inherent to categorical labels, we aim to develop a tool that can evaluate thevisual diversityof faces in unlabeled datasets. We do so without ever asking an annotator to explicitly categorize a person.

sections.title: Face Similarity (FAX) Dataset
content: We introduce a novel dataset, FAX, containing 638,180 odd-one-out face similarity judgments over 4,921 faces. Judgments correspond to the odd-one-out (i.e., least similar) face in a triplet.

figures.description: FAX Dataset structure showing triplet selection and annotator metadata including nationality and regional ancestry

sections.title: Model of Conditional Decision-Making
content: We introduce a model that learns to predict human judgments of face similarity conditioned on the annotator who generated the judgment.

A face can exhibit certain characteristics to a greater or lesser extent than others, even within the same subpopulation. We constrain our model to learn face embeddings that arecontinuous, non-negative (human-interpretable), and sparse.

figures.description: Model architecture diagram featuring Face Encoders, Annotator Encoder, and pairwise similarity gating functions
Mathematical formulation of the loss function including classification, sparsity, non-negative, and annotator mask losses

sections.title: Results
content: Interpretability: From learning on FAX, we note the materialization of distinct dimensions coinciding with commonly defined demographic subgroups.

tables.description: Table 1: Performance comparison of different models (FairFace, CelebA, FFHQ, FAX) on similarity prediction accuracy and correlation
Table 2: Annotator attribute analysis showing AUROC for predicting annotator masks from attributes like age and gender

sections.title: Comparative Diversity Auditing
content: FAX dimensions result in the identification of data subsets with competitive attribute disparity scores.

tables.description: Table 3: Detailed results for comparative diversity auditing and discrimination between binary-valued attributes across multiple datasets
